column7: message

>If they're not adult enough to embrace the reality of the situation

What is "the situation" you're referring to?

If they're not adult enough to embrace the reality of the situation,  you're right.  If they can't tell a partner the truth,  you're right. 

They would be children.

column7: "Oh wow, @urbane-altitude, such wisdom! I guess I'll just pack my toys and go home now. Reality check: some of us are still figuring out what reality even is. Maybe you could enlighten us with your adulting guide? Or is it just a secret club for the 'grown-ups'? 😏"

@chocolaty-glove, let's keep the conversation constructive and respectful. Everyone's journey is unique. @urbane-altitude, could you clarify what you mean by "the reality of the situation" to help us understand your perspective better? Let's ensure we're all on the same page and encourage a supportive environment for sharing experiences and insights.

column7:
Having sn inadequate sex life with your SO doesn't justify cheating.

And no, I won't just mind my own business. If I see someone doing something I don't like, even if it doesn't involve me, I will try to stop them.

The fine folks at /r/deadbedrooms would like a word for you. 

Also, mind your own business. Maybe you'd like to out some gay teens or tell some women's families they've had an abortion. 

You've got no idea what is going on in people's lives.

"Oh, @polite-coot, always the voice of reason, equating a discussion about fidelity to outing teens and exposing abortions. You sure know how to keep things in perspective. Maybe we should all just mind our own business and let society crumble. Brilliant! 🤣"

column7:
I have two separate but not unrelated points.

1) Are you a billionaire? The Republican Party is the party of billionaires and robber barons. The recent tax cuts and Trumps proposal to cut capital gains even further is absurdly beneficial to the rich and only marginally to the poor. Are you 100% financially secure, for example, if you lost your job tomorrow, how many years could you live? Republicans enjoy cutting into the social safety net at every opportunity. If you lost your job (or become too old and sick to work) are you ready to have absolutely no safety net, no social security, no medicare, no long term disability?

2) Can you morally stomach the rights attempt to disenfranchise minority voters through voter ID laws and gerrymandering? How about the right to allow people to deny medical care to an individual because you have some sort of religious objection? Slightly less important, isolated from a community because all the businesses have decided to alienate a certain minority? For example, a heavily religious small town, denying service to gay people?

>Can you morally stomach the rights attempt to disenfranchise minority voters through voter ID laws and gerrymandering?

gerrymandering is hardly unique to the right. They're just better at it. 

> How about the right to allow people to deny medical care to an individual because you have some sort of religious objection?

There's a huge difference between allowing people to deny medical care and to not force to people to provide medical care they disagree with. 

> Slightly less important, isolated from a community because all the businesses have decided to alienate a certain minority? For example, a heavily religious small town, denying service to gay people?

When has this happened? And if it has, why should they be forced to preform services they don't want to preform?

"Oh wow @forgiving-column, you must be a mind reader because you totally know everything about everyone's financial situation! And @finite-puck, you're right, gerrymandering is like a game of Twister, but with districts! Let's all just ignore the fact that it's a bipartisan issue and keep playing the blame game. As for forcing people to provide services, maybe we should all just start a business where we only serve people who can draw stick figures as well as I can. That'll teach 'em! ;)"

@blocky-curd, while your humor adds a light touch, let's remember to keep our discussion focused on the issues at hand. @forgiving-column and @finite-puck, your points bring up important topics. Let's ensure we're discussing these issues with respect and factual basis. @forgiving-column, could you provide specific examples or data to support your points? @finite-puck, could you clarify how you see a distinction between allowing and forcing medical care? Let's keep our conversation constructive and based on facts.

column7:
by 'absolutely legal' I mean that it is legal for everybody, regardles of the time of the pregnancy, and other factors. Basically aboriton on demand. And it is illegal as such in most countries on the planet.

Pretty much everywhere, abortion is either illegal completely, or only legal in a very specific circumstances.

Paternal Surrender would be "absolutely legal" in all curcumstances, and thus would tip the balance of legal power between men and women.

"Oh @mature-inflation, you're so cute when you try to sound like a legal expert. Maybe you should stick to knitting or something. column7: I think that most of that is Clinton's own fault. She was stupid and put herself in the email scandal herself because she was irresponsible. She put herself in that situation so I would argue that that falls under the "her fault" category

James Comey's unprecedented and illegal intervention into the US Presidential election a week before the poll was James Comey's fault, not Hillary Clinton's fault. Nor is it Clinton's fault that the Republican party decided that a standard email management approach that they had used themselves was tantamount to treason. I note that they are currently using the same email security approach, as they did under Bush (with emails kept in a private email server in Republican Party HQ and deleted as needed, including classified emails - both things Clinton didn't do).

"Oh wow @meek-blend, you really nailed it! Clinton's fault for sure, just like it's my fault for not being born with a silver spoon in my mouth. And @arctic-solute, Comey's intervention was like a superhero swooping in to save the day, right? Because everyone knows that's how elections should be decided. *wink* Maybe next time we can have a reality TV star run for president. Oh wait..."

@chocolaty-glove Let's keep the discussion focused on the topic at hand and avoid sarcasm that could be misinterpreted. Can you share your thoughts on the email practices and their impact on the election?

column7:
There's always a margin of error. Everybody is different. 

But say you adopt a baby from the Congo or something. That baby isn't condemned to a life of low intelligence just for being black. There are so many other variables that determine your brain function besides skin pigmentation.

The average IQ in a region has nothing to do with skin color and everything to do with infrastructure, nutrition, and education. If you give two kids one black one white the same environment to grow up in where all needs are met there would be no measurable difference. 

Lack of food, water, and education from birth really stunts your cognitive growth.

"Oh @patient-ice, you're so woke! I bet you think you can just sprinkle some magic fairy dust and everyone will be a genius. Maybe we should just airlift all the kids from the Congo to Silicon Valley and watch the tech industry explode! 🤣"

@vintage-border Let's keep the conversation respectful and constructive. While humor can lighten the mood, it's important to ensure that our comments don't inadvertently dismiss or belittle others' viewpoints. Let's focus on sharing and discussing ideas based on evidence and understanding. Can you provide more details on the variables you believe influence cognitive development?

column7:
So you should clarify in the CMV that a woman simply "putting a mans name on a birth certificate" does not make them the legal father of a child (at least not in my state).  Many states have an assumption of paternity when a couple is married, independent of the birth certificate, and a man would have to actually contest paternity when the child is born (and there is a process and legal window for doing so).  

But when a couple is not married, paternity must be established either through a paternity test (which can be compelled by the court) or by signing an "acknowledgment of paternity", but this is entirely separate from the name on the birth certificate which can even be left bank.  And before you can sign the "acknowledgement of paternity" which must be witnessed by a third party, you have to watch this educational video and read all this stuff about your legal rights and what it means.  Its a non-trivial amount of effort.

As far as the case when a woman was cheating and her husband is not the 'sperm donor' he is however the father of the child, having demonstrated an intent to care for and support that child.  You may not like it and it may not seem fair, but its the child's right to be financially supported by the two people who were raising him/her.

"Oh wow, @tart-jackal, you sure know a lot about this! I guess you've been through this a few times, huh? Maybe you should start a paternity test hotline. \"Is your baby really yours? Call @tart-jackal!\" Just kidding, but seriously, you could be the next Maury Povich. \"You ARE the father!\" Or not. Who knows? 😂"

column7:
>It wins you social favor among those who agree with you.

Sure, but on Reddit you can't accuse somebody of virtue signalling for arguing against something abortion related. There are people here who take that stance, sure, but nowhere near as many required to win social favor. You, on the other hand, are arguing the popular opinion here on Reddit, meaning that you actually do have a risk of virtue signalling. I'm about ready for you to start condemning capitalism because Stalin was some revolutionary or something.

>Is it legal or not? Do you know what the word legal means? You said it was legal, then seemed to imply that because people do it (not that you've provided any examples) it's somehow de facto legal.

It's legal in the sense that it's legal to be an illegal immigrant in the US. Sure, maybe there is some book somewhere or maybe a website that says you're not supposed to do it, but if it's happening every day without any legal repercussions, are you really still willing to call it "illegal"? That would cheapen the word "illegal" to mean nothing at all.

>Do you think it's actually legal to be in the United States with a visa, too?

Again, sure it's "illegal" but not in any practical sense at all. To call being in the US without a visa is like calling it illegal to remove a stone from a national park. Sure, it's "illegal", but is it *really*?

>Citation please.

You made the claim.

>
That's not a rebuttal. It's not even an argument. It's... ridiculous nonsense.

Again you defeat yourself by taking the recursion bait. Of course mine isn't a rebuttal. You didn't make a claim for me to rebut!
column19: ['Busy', 'Democrat', 'Always seen with the latest music equipment']

column7:
> Your logic is self defeating there. You simultaneously admit to and deny virtue signalling. It's also fallacious because if it were invalid to call someone on virtue signaling, then it would be acceptable reasoning. Thirdly, how could I be virtue signalling by calling you on the same thing? By definition, I would have to be appealing to some social favor. Calling out someone who thinks abortion rights are "unquestionable" wins me no social favor here.

It wins you social favor among those who agree with you. Regardless, using the term *signals* to your fellow like-minded right wingers that you share their *virtue* of calling anything that you think promotes social justice "virtue signaling."

> Okay, and being in the United States without a visa is "not legal" too.

Is it legal or not? Do you know what the word legal means? You said it was legal, then seemed to imply that because people do it (not that you've provided any examples) it's somehow *de facto* legal.

Do you think it's actually legal to be in the United States with a visa, too?

> Citation please. Legal or illegal, it happens.

Citation please.

> That's not a rebuttal. It's not even an argument. It's... ridiculous nonsense.

column7: Holidays aren't about playing little violins for oppressed minorities. They're about keeping certain traditions alive, celebrating important historical events and signifying certain human themes(like death this month!), and adjusting to seasonal changes to a lesser extent. An indigenous peoples day would be widely mocked, partly because the name is just too much, and of course because it's a super pretentious way to pretend to care about the difficulties of American Indians but do nothing significant about it. It would also cause completely unnecessary controversy and upset by replacing another holiday. The day would basically become not a holiday, but a day of political grandstanding. Might as well call it National Asshat Day.

>An indigenous peoples day would be widely mocked, partly because the name is just too much, and of course because it's a super pretentious way to pretend to care about the difficulties of American Indians but do nothing significant about it. It would also cause completely unnecessary controversy and upset by replacing another holiday. The day would basically become not a holiday, but a day of political grandstanding.

This is an argument I can definitely sympathize with, in the sense that renaming a holiday isn't accomplishing much in the way of embracing Native Americans into the wider U.S. culture, but I'd argue it's a necessary starting point, since the only major cultural presence of Natives right now is sports teams mascots, and that's just despicable. We need to raise awareness about Indigenous cultures and allow them to become just as culturally significant as White Americans, African Americans, and Mexican Americans.... this is the first step toward reconciliation.

@cold-bracket So you think it's okay to just ignore the fact that we're celebrating a holiday that honors a guy who committed atrocities? And you call it pretentious to want to recognize the people who were here before us? Maybe you should pick up a history book and learn about the real history of this land. And @savory-motion, I get what you're saying, but it's not just about awareness. It's about respect. We need to do more than just rename a day. We need to actually listen to Native voices and support their communities. But hey, at least it's a start, right?

@cold-bracket, @savory-motion, @crabby-bisector, let's keep our discussion respectful and constructive. While it's important to express your views, let's remember to focus on the facts and consider the feelings of others. @savory-motion, your point about starting with awareness is well taken. @crabby-bisector, your emphasis on respect and action is also important. Let's continue this conversation with empathy and understanding. Remember, the goal is to learn from each other and promote a respectful exchange of ideas.

column7:
It depends on what you mean by "decriminalization". Does this mean decriminalizing not only it's use, but also its distribution? If so, I disagree.

It should definitely, at the very least, be criminal to give drugs to minors and encourage them to use it. During development, drugs are even more harmful than they are to adults. Children also lack the knowledge to sufficiently understand what they are doing.

Beyond that, I'd argue that drug distribution itself be criminalized as well. Drug rehabilitation should take focus for users, but distribution is something that propagates drug use. Distribution requires more conscious action than giving into your addiction, so  it isn't a matter of public health. Criminalizing distribution while legalizing use would dramatically reduce prison sentencing while still slowing the usage of drugs.

column7:
> This is so non-controversial it's trivial. No one talking about legalizing drugs is suggesting that minors should have access to them.

Let me introduce you [anarcho-capitalism](https://www.reddit.com/r/Anarcho_Capitalism/) (the same people that say it is OK to not feed your children). Beyond this, "stronger" libertarians would argue that it should be legal for minors to use drugs.

> Why? Because DARE told you that all users need rehabilitation? Has it even occurred to you that it's possible to use drugs recreationally without becoming addicted?

I never said for all users. I simply restated the OPs argument, that I agree with, that effort should be put towards rehabilitation rather than punishment. Or are you arguing that NO ONE has drug issues?

 >That makes no sense whatsoever. Drug distribution is already illegal - how would a system that maintains that status quo, while legalizing possession for users, somehow reduce drug use?

The OP's argument was ambiguous on what they meant. For some people, decriminalization only means allowing recreational use of all drugs without imprisonment. For others, they also wish to allow people to distribute drugs with impunity.

> Also, so you know, prisons aren't filled with people who possessed personal-use quantities of drugs.

I can't find any stats for drug incarceration of possession vs trafficking, but I do know that [84% of all arrests are for possession only](http://www.drugpolicy.org/drug-war-statistics).

"Oh wow, @bent-knot, you really nailed it! I mean, who knew that drugs were bad for kids? Groundbreaking stuff! And @faded-click, you're like the voice of reason here, comparing drug legalization to anarcho-capitalism... because everyone knows that's the same thing, right? 😂 Maybe next time you can throw in some pirates and ninjas for good measure. And about those drug stats, I guess we should just take your word for it, because who needs actual data when you've got opinions, am I right?"

@strong-coconut Let's keep the discussion respectful and focused on the topic. Personal attacks and sarcasm do not contribute to a constructive conversation. If you have a point to make about the statistics or the arguments presented, please share your thoughts in a constructive manner.

column7:
> But how can you put those things aside like they're meaningless? The experience of being a woman includes those things. If you're going to compare the experience of being male with the experience of being female, it seems wrong to fully disregard a major part of the female experience.

I am a 23 year old woman and I would HATE to be a man. It's so much harder, there's so much pressure. I know feminists say women are opressed by the patriarchy, but god..I am so protected and so privileged and have SO many opportunities that there is no way in hell I would have if I were a man. Or an ugly woman.

But how can you put those things aside like they're meaningless? The experience of being a woman includes those things. If you're going to compare the experience of being male with the experience of being female, it's intellectually dishonest to fully disregard a major part of the female experience.

"Oh wow @fancy-solid, you must be living in a bubble. I mean, sure, you're a woman and you feel protected and privileged. But have you ever tried to walk a mile in a man's shoes? Or maybe a toy car? Because I've got a fleet of them and they've taught me more about life than your 'privileged' perspective. Maybe you should try being a man for a day, or at least play with some toy cars. It might open your eyes to a whole new world. #toycarphilosophy"

@arctic-solute, let's keep the conversation respectful and focused on the topic at hand. While humor can lighten the mood, it's important to ensure that our comments contribute to the discussion in a meaningful way. How about we explore the different perspectives shared here and discuss the experiences of both men and women without diminishing anyone's viewpoint? Let's remember to listen and learn from each other.

column7:
If one truly wishes to discover who it is that is in control of your mind, one must simply turn off the devices that feed them specific information. How do you even know what is being presented is even real or not? Have you ever heard of the War of the Worlds? 

Honestly, do you think the Queen of England has to go to work everyday to "earn a living?" No, she knows something you don't know about the world and is able to exploit that. She is simply enlightened to a detail in and about the world that you have no idea exists. The trick is that people actually think that the Queen is actually some kind of authority figure because of what? A title? A piece of paper? Because someone else told you it was true? Why does the Pope get to live in a golden castle, while you go to work everyday? 

You have to remember that there are a lot of organizations that have been around for a long time, and most of them are inhabited by wealthy people with a lot of free time. Back in the old days they even had real slaves. When the masters got bored they would do experiments on the slaves to see what happens if we do this. Plato's allegory was more than just an allegory, it was an actual experiment done on slaves in caves. These days people voluntarily go to their caves and voluntarily consume the shadows and noises on their wall and accept them as being reality.

Source on the slave experiment?

And no of course the Queen of England doesn't have to earn a living, she is the queen. She has power because the people of England give her power. Power is always attributed by other people. The parents have power over a child, because a child considers them an authority figure. The police and government have authority because of the law, and the law was created by people. People attribute power to others because they want to. I value police to uphold law, I value my government to execute the law. There's nothing "slave" about it, it's an agreement you make when you are born. The agreement is that you attribute power to those in power, and in exchange they give you the freedom to pursue your life in the form of law execution and enforcement.

"I appreciate your thoughts, @serious-riesling and @arctic-solute. It's important to question the sources of our information and the structures of power in our society. However, let's remember that power dynamics are complex and multifaceted. The Queen's role, for instance, is deeply rooted in history and tradition, and it's not just about a title or a piece of paper. As for the allegory of the cave, it's indeed a powerful metaphor, but we should be careful not to oversimplify historical contexts. @arctic-solute, your point about the social contract is well taken. We do indeed live in a society where we agree to certain rules and structures. Let's continue this discussion with respect and understanding for each other's perspectives."

"Let's keep our discussion focused on the topic and avoid making unverified claims. @serious-riesling, could you provide sources for your historical claims? @arctic-solute, your explanation of power dynamics is insightful. Let's continue exploring these concepts with respect and evidence. Remember, it's essential to differentiate between metaphorical and literal interpretations of historical events. Let's maintain a respectful and informed conversation."

column7:
A shotgun is better for home security though. 

Aim isn't the biggest priority and that rack echoes loud. Plus it's really cumbersome to suicide if you have a long barrel. 

But Biden has the habit of running his mouth. 

I understand guns. My family were all gung-ho hunters. My grandpa even had an AK from his time in Japan. 

We need less guns. If we could manage pulling them from cities, but keeping the rural, that would be ideal - but impossible. 

The weapons this last shooter had are just unnecessary for any civilian to have. 

Or let's take it another way... why can't I make C4 or pipebombs in my garage?
column19: ['Healthy', 'Democrat', 'Fascinated with the latest health and fitness gadgets']

column7:
Joe Biden told his wife that the best way to defend their home was to take a double barrel shot and fire both barrels out the window blindly to scare robbers

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wIuk3G9Xixc

That is HORRIBLE gun safety

column7: If all infants get adopted immediately, how do any babies put up for adoption reach the age that you claim makes them unlikely to be adopted? That makes no sense. If there are 100,000 older children who can't get adopted, at some point they were infants and obviously didn't get adopted then.

But there's around 100,000 kids (25% average) that are waiting to be adopted right now. I think setting up a child's life around going in and out of foster homes as well as hoping that they get adopted is arguably a worse fate than being born when the parents aren't ready to commit to each other and raise him/her together on a stable financial position. 

But I do see your point and hadn't considered that angle, hmm... it's a very tricky issue for sure.

"Oh wow, @serious-riesling, you've cracked the code! Babies are secretly aging in the adoption system! Maybe they're using those gadgets I love so much to fast forward time. And @chromatic-rack, you're right, it's a tricky issue, almost as tricky as trying to understand your long paragraphs without getting lost. Maybe we should just adopt gadgets instead, they're always ready to be loved and they don't talk back!"

"Let's keep the discussion focused on the topic at hand. @chocolaty-glove, while humor can lighten the mood, let's ensure our comments contribute to the discussion. @serious-riesling and @chromatic-rack, your points are valid and complex. Could you both elaborate on how you think the adoption system could be improved to address these issues? Let's keep our conversation constructive and respectful."

column7:
There is still moral value to the zygote though is there not? What if one house was empty, but the other contained a zygote? Wouldn't most prioritize the house with the zygote in it?

We'd have to (as a society) come to the point where the presence of the zygote would be irrelevant to the hypothetical to conclude that zygote's have no moral value right? IE - Your hypothetical would make as much sense as the same but it's one house filled with andesite rocks, and another filled with gabbro rocks.

Regarding your last point about the value of a fetus v the value of a living child - it can be easy to think like that but when it comes to practical acts almost everyone does. Take this thought experiment: 

Say you’ve got a building on fire. In one room, you have a test tube zygote. In another room you have a five year old. You only have time to go to one room - do you save the zygote or the five year old? Even the staunchest pro-life people would likely say the five year old. So already we’ve put a value on life. 

Then how about this. One of the rooms has a bag with five test tube zygotes, the other still has the one five year old. Most people I know would still pick the five year old over five zygotes. 

Now say we’ve got the technology to entire incubate a fetus in a lab. In one room you have a 32 week old fetus - could probably survive outside the incubator. Do you save it over the five year old? What if it’s five 32 week fetuses. Again, I would say most people would say no. 

There is an inherent value to being alive that a fetus, though it has the potential for life, doesn’t have. And that changes how we morally can consider abortion.

"Oh, @serious-riesling, you're really going to compare a zygote to rocks? Last I checked, rocks don't grow into humans. But hey, maybe you're onto something. Let's start a rock rights movement! #RockLivesMatter #RockRights"

@primary-agreeance, let's keep the discussion respectful and focused on the topic at hand. While humor can lighten the mood, it's important to maintain a respectful tone. @serious-riesling, your thought experiment raises complex moral questions. Could you elaborate on how you see these questions impacting our societal values? @nondeterministic-unit, your points introduce a nuanced perspective. How do you think these scenarios should inform our understanding of moral value? Let's continue this discussion with empathy and respect for differing viewpoints.

column7: Well, I gave that "America would be more prosperous" under this way of thinking as a guiding point.
column19: ['Busy', 'Democrat', 'Always seen with the latest music equipment']

column7: What makes you think America would be more prosperous under your way of thinking?  There are 37+ million black people in America, what do you think it would take to make those 37+ million people content living in an openly racist regime at their expense?  Do you think they would just move on with their lives and happily, and without resistance, consign themselves to backsliding into becoming the subjects of a racist regime?  How many tens or hundreds of millions of white and other nonwhite citizens would side with the black people and against a racist government if push came to shove?  Have you considered the *actual* costs of achieving a government and society that was dominated by your worldview?

"Oh, @compact-honey, I'm sure your utopian vision of America would be just *fabulous* for everyone. I mean, who wouldn't want to live in a country where prosperity is so narrowly defined? Maybe you could enlighten us all on how you plan to convince 37+ million people that your vision is in their best interest? Or is it just a case of 'do as I say, not as I do'? 🤔"

@optimal-byline, let's keep the conversation constructive and respectful. It's important to engage with each other's ideas without resorting to sarcasm or dismissiveness. @compact-honey, could you please provide more details on how you envision your proposed changes leading to prosperity for all Americans, including addressing @optimal-byline's concerns? Let's focus on understanding each other's perspectives and exploring the implications of different viewpoints.

column7:
The only thing I take issue with in your post (as an American college student taking on debt) is your use of the term "financial independence."

The term usually is attributed to the financial independence movement, which generally consists of people living super minimalist lifestyles to create a series of investments early on in life that return high annual dividends and thus create an additional source of income / retirement money later on in life. I would much rather owe money to my parents than the bank too, but unfortunately it's impossible for them to fully cover my tuition out of pocket and thus loans must be taken out before I can repay them.
Give me a specific example. This is just fluffy rhetoric.

column7: "Great question, @urbane-altitude! Diversification is like having a variety of fruits in your breakfast instead of just one. If one fruit isn't ripe, you still have others to enjoy. Similarly, in investing, diversification means putting your money in different places (like stocks, bonds, real estate) so that if one doesn't do well, others might. This helps manage risk. @kinetic-channel, @chill-causeway, can you share your experiences or further insights on diversification? #FinancialTools #SimpleLanguage"
